results.possibleIdentity: Robert Grossman was an engineer active in the late 1960s and early 1970s who was based in Union, New Jersey. He was a professional technical member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg to foster collaborations between artists and engineers. According to historical E.A.T. member records and directories from 1968, Grossman was affiliated with the Lockheed Electronics Company, located in Plainfield/Watchung, New Jersey. His technical expertise was recorded as focusing on computer sciences and control systems. During this period, Lockheed Electronics was a major defense and aerospace contractor in the Union County area, specializing in advanced electronic systems, radar, and flight controls. His participation in the E.A.T. Technical Services registry placed him among a community of engineers from nearby facilities like Bell Laboratories and Singer-Kearfott who volunteered their skills for experimental artistic projects.

Robert L. Grossman (born circa 1958) is a prominent computer scientist and professor who specializes in data science, predictive analytics, and control systems. He currently serves as the Frederick H. Rawson Distinguished Service Professor in Medicine and Computer Science at the University of Chicago. While he is a leading expert in 'control systems' and 'computer science' (having edited works such as 'Symbolic Computation: Applications to Scientific Computing' for SIAM in 1989), his professional career began later than the late 1960s. He earned his A.B. from Harvard in 1980 and his Ph.D. from Princeton in 1985. Much of his early research focused on nonlinear control systems and the application of computer algebra to engineering problems. Although he grew up in Northern New Jersey and has ties to the region's technical community, he is likely a younger individual or relative of the Robert Grossman active in the 1960s E.A.T. movement.
